enrichment.short_description: SITE123 is a beginner-friendly, all-in-one website builder that lets small businesses and individuals create a professional website, online store, or blog without writing code.
enrichment.seo_title: SITE123 Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SITE123 is an Israeli no-code website builder for small businesses. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons, and how it compares to competitors.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SITE123 is a cloud-based, no-code website builder founded in 2015 and headquartered in Beersheba, Israel. The platform is designed for small business owners, freelancers, and individuals who have no design or coding experience and want to launch a website quickly. Users pick a template based on their business category, such as restaurant, portfolio, online store, or blog, and then customize it through a simplified guided editor rather than a fully freeform canvas, which keeps the learning curve low compared with builders like Wix or Squarespace. SITE123 includes a free-forever plan with a SITE123 subdomain and limited storage, plus paid Premium tiers, referred to as Basic, Advanced, Professional, and Gold, that unlock a custom domain, additional storage and bandwidth, ecommerce functionality, email accounts, and removal of SITE123 branding. Premium pricing starts around $12.80 per month when billed on a long-term plan, with a 14-day money-back guarantee on paid plans. Built-in features include basic SEO tools such as meta tags and sitemap generation, mobile-responsive design, a blogging module, an online store with payment gateway support, contact forms, and 24/7 customer support with website-building assistance. SITE123 operates with a small team of roughly 11-50 employees and has grown for a decade without taking on public venture funding, suggesting a bootstrapped, profitable operating model. It positions itself against Wix, GoDaddy Website Builder, and Weebly as a lower-cost, simplicity-first alternative for beginners.

long_form_blocks.heading: SITE123 Overview
paragraphs: SITE123 is a website builder launched in 2015 out of Beersheba, Israel, built specifically for people with no coding or design background. Rather than a blank drag-and-drop canvas, it starts users with an industry-specific template and a guided setup wizard that walks through pages, colors, and content step by step.

The platform targets solo entrepreneurs, local service businesses, and hobbyists who want a working website fast rather than a highly customized one. This trade-off, simplicity over flexibility, is central to how SITE123 differentiates itself from more open-ended builders like Wix or Webflow.

After a decade of operation with no public funding rounds, SITE123 appears to run as a bootstrapped, profitable company with a small team, focused on serving a large volume of small-business customers globally through freemium pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SITE123
paragraphs: Every SITE123 site includes free hosting, a free SSL certificate, and mobile-responsive templates out of the box, so users do not need to configure infrastructure separately. The editor supports blogging, contact forms, and basic image galleries alongside the core page builder.

Paid plans add an online store module with support for accepting payments, letting small businesses sell physical or digital products without a separate ecommerce platform. Basic SEO controls, including editable meta titles, descriptions, and automatic sitemap generation, are built into the editor.

Support is a notable focus for SITE123: the company offers 24/7 live chat and a hands-on website-building assistance service, which is unusual among budget website builders and aimed at users who need extra guidance.

long_form_blocks.heading: SITE123 Pricing Plans
paragraphs: SITE123 offers a genuinely free-forever plan that includes a SITE123 subdomain, limited storage and bandwidth, and core editing features, making it possible to build and publish a basic site at no cost.

Paid Premium plans, marketed under names like Basic, Advanced, Professional, and Gold, unlock a custom domain connection, expanded storage and bandwidth, ecommerce functionality, and removal of SITE123 branding. Premium pricing starts at roughly $12.80 per month when paid on a longer-term plan, with shorter commitments costing more per month.
All paid plans come with a 14-day money-back guarantee, letting customers try premium features with limited financial risk before committing long term.

features.title: Guided setup wizard
features.description: A step-by-step onboarding flow picks a template based on business type and walks new users through core setup instead of an open-ended canvas.

features.title: Free hosting and SSL
features.description: Every site, including free-tier sites, is hosted by SITE123 with a free SSL certificate included by default.

features.title: Built-in online store
features.description: Paid plans include ecommerce tools for listing products and accepting payments without a separate platform.

features.title: Mobile-responsive templates
features.description: Industry-specific templates automatically adapt layouts for mobile and tablet screens.

features.title: Basic SEO tools
features.description: Editable meta titles and descriptions plus automatic sitemap generation help pages get indexed.

features.title: Blog module
features.description: A built-in blogging tool lets users publish posts without a separate CMS.

features.title: Multilingual site support
features.description: SITE123 supports building sites intended for non-English-speaking audiences alongside English.

features.title: 24/7 live chat support
features.description: Round-the-clock support includes hands-on assistance for users building their first website.

pros: Very easy for absolute beginners to get a site live quickly
Genuine free-forever plan, not just a time-limited trial
Affordable entry-level premium pricing versus Wix or Squarespace
Built-in ecommerce without a separate plugin or platform
Responsive 24/7 customer support
cons: Less design flexibility than freeform builders like Webflow or Wix
SITE123 branding remains visible on the free plan
Fewer advanced features and integrations than larger competitors
Storage and bandwidth caps persist even on paid tiers
Smaller third-party app and plugin ecosystem

faqs.question: Is SITE123 free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, SITE123 offers a free-forever plan with a SITE123 subdomain and limited storage, though a custom domain and full features require a paid Premium plan.

faqs.question: What does SITE123 Premium include?
faqs.answer: Premium plans add a custom domain connection, more storage and bandwidth, ecommerce functionality, additional email accounts, and removal of SITE123 branding.

faqs.question: Does SITE123 support online stores?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans include a built-in store module for accepting payments and listing products.

faqs.question: Can I connect my own domain to SITE123?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom domain connection is available on Premium plans, with domain registration available from roughly $10.80 per month on an annual plan.

faqs.question: Is SITE123 good for SEO?
faqs.answer: SITE123 includes basic SEO tools like editable meta tags and automatic sitemap generation, though it lacks the advanced SEO controls of dedicated SEO platforms.

faqs.question: Does SITE123 offer a refund?
faqs.answer: Yes, paid plans come with a 14-day money-back guarantee.

faqs.question: Where is SITE123 based?
faqs.answer: SITE123 was founded in 2015 and is headquartered in Beersheba, Israel.

faqs.question: Who is SITE123 best suited for?
faqs.answer: SITE123 is aimed at small businesses, freelancers, and individuals who want a simple website without design or coding experience.

enrichment.short_description: Sitebulb is a desktop and cloud technical SEO auditing and website crawling tool built in the UK, used by SEO professionals and agencies to find and prioritize site issues.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (desktop license tiers plus cloud crawl tiers)
enrichment.seo_title: Sitebulb Review 2026: Pricing, Features and How It Compares
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sitebulb is a UK-built desktop and cloud SEO crawler. Compare 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons for this technical SEO auditing tool.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sitebulb is a technical SEO auditing and website crawling tool founded in 2017 by Gareth Brown and Patrick Hathaway, and headquartered in Biddenden, Kent, in the United Kingdom. It is built specifically for SEO professionals, consultants, and agencies who need to audit websites for crawlability, indexability, on-page, and technical SEO issues. Unlike some enterprise-only crawlers, Sitebulb ships as a desktop application for Windows and Mac, called Sitebulb Desktop, as well as a browser-based Sitebulb Cloud product, giving users the choice between running audits locally or offloading large crawls to hosted infrastructure. The desktop Lite plan starts at roughly $13.50 per month when billed annually, about $17.50 month-to-month, and supports up to 10,000 URLs per crawl across five projects, while the Pro plan costs around $35 per month and raises the crawl limit to 500,000 URLs. Sitebulb Cloud plans range from roughly $65 to $245 per month depending on crawl volume, with the largest cloud tier supporting audits of up to 10 million URLs. The product is known in the SEO community for its visual, prioritized audit reports that explain issues in plain language with actionable hints, its site-wide visualizations such as crawl maps and sunburst diagrams, and its Google Data Studio and API integrations. Sitebulb remains a small, privately held UK company with a team of roughly 2 to 10 employees, positioning it as a founder-led alternative to larger crawlers like Screaming Frog, DeepCrawl, and Botify.

long_form_blocks.heading: Sitebulb Overview
paragraphs: Sitebulb was founded in 2017 by Gareth Brown and Patrick Hathaway and is based in Biddenden, Kent, United Kingdom. It was built to make technical SEO audits more approachable, pairing raw crawl data with plain-language, prioritized recommendations rather than leaving practitioners to interpret spreadsheets unaided.

The product ships in two forms: Sitebulb Desktop, a native app for Windows and Mac that crawls sites using the user's own machine and bandwidth, and Sitebulb Cloud, a browser-based version that runs crawls on hosted infrastructure for larger sites or teams that need shared access.

Sitebulb remains a small, independently run company with a team of roughly 2 to 10 people, and it has built a strong reputation within the SEO community as a founder-led alternative to larger enterprise crawling platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Sitebulb
paragraphs: Sitebulb's audits go beyond raw data by attaching a prioritized 'Hint' to each issue found, explaining what it is, why it matters, and how to fix it, which makes the tool accessible to SEOs who are newer to technical auditing.

The tool renders JavaScript during crawls to reflect what modern search engines actually see, and it generates visualizations such as crawl maps and sunburst diagrams to help users understand site architecture and internal linking at a glance.

Sitebulb supports scheduled recurring audits, integrations with Google Analytics and Google Search Console, white-label PDF reporting for agencies, and API access on higher-tier plans for automating audits into other workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Sitebulb Pricing Plans
paragraphs: Sitebulb Desktop is sold in two tiers: Lite, starting around $13.50 per month billed annually with a 10,000 URL crawl limit across five projects, and Pro, around $35 per month, which raises the crawl limit to 500,000 URLs.

Sitebulb Cloud is priced separately from the desktop app and scales with crawl volume, starting around $65 per month and reaching approximately $245 per month for the top tier supporting up to 10 million URLs per audit.
A free trial is available, letting prospective customers test crawl and reporting features before committing to either the desktop or cloud subscription tiers.

features.description: Choose between a local desktop crawler or hosted Sitebulb Cloud infrastructure depending on site size and team needs.

features.description: Sunburst diagrams and crawl maps visualize site architecture and internal linking structure.

features.title: JavaScript rendering support
features.description: Crawls can render JavaScript to reflect what modern search engines actually index.

features.title: Scheduled recurring audits
features.description: Audits can be scheduled to run automatically on a recurring basis to track SEO health over time.

features.title: Analytics and Search Console integration
features.description: Connects to Google Analytics and Google Search Console to layer performance data onto crawl findings.

features.title: White-label PDF reporting
features.description: Agencies can generate branded PDF reports to share technical SEO findings with clients.

features.title: API access
features.description: Higher-tier plans expose an API for automating crawls and pulling data into other systems.

pros: Beginner-friendly explanations of technical SEO issues
Detailed, visual site architecture reporting
Flexible desktop or cloud deployment options
Affordable entry price for freelancers and solo consultants
Strong reputation among SEO professionals and agencies
cons: Desktop crawls are limited by local machine resources
Cloud tiers become expensive at high URL volumes
Full feature set has a steeper learning curve than basic crawlers
Smaller integration ecosystem than larger enterprise platforms
No fully free tier beyond the trial period

faqs.question: Is Sitebulb free?
faqs.answer: Sitebulb offers a free trial but requires a paid subscription for ongoing use, split between Desktop and Cloud pricing tiers.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Sitebulb Desktop and Cloud?
faqs.answer: Desktop runs crawls locally on the user's machine, while Cloud runs crawls on hosted infrastructure accessible from a browser, better suited for large sites or teams.

faqs.question: How many URLs can Sitebulb crawl?
faqs.answer: Desktop plans support up to 500,000 URLs per crawl on the Pro tier, while Cloud plans can support audits of up to 10 million URLs.

faqs.question: Does Sitebulb render JavaScript?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sitebulb can render JavaScript during crawls to reflect how modern search engines see JavaScript-heavy pages.

faqs.question: Is Sitebulb suitable for agencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sitebulb includes white-label PDF reporting and API access, both commonly used by SEO agencies serving multiple clients.

faqs.question: How does Sitebulb compare to Screaming Frog?
faqs.answer: Sitebulb emphasizes prioritized, explained audit hints and visualizations, while Screaming Frog is often used as a raw, highly configurable crawl data tool; many SEOs use both.

faqs.question: Who founded Sitebulb?
faqs.answer: Sitebulb was founded in 2017 by Gareth Brown and Patrick Hathaway in the United Kingdom.

faqs.question: Does Sitebulb offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sitebulb offers a free trial before requiring a paid Desktop or Cloud subscription.

use_cases.title: Freelance SEO consultant auditing client sites
use_cases.description: A solo consultant uses Sitebulb Desktop Lite to run technical audits and generate reports for a handful of client websites.

use_cases.title: In-house team running recurring technical audits

use_cases.description: An SEO agency uses Sitebulb Cloud and white-label PDF reporting to deliver branded audits across multiple client accounts.

features: Desktop app for Windows and Mac
Desktop app for Windows and Mac
Up to 10 million URLs per audit

enrichment.short_description: SiteGround is a Bulgaria-based web hosting company offering shared, cloud, and WordPress-optimized hosting to millions of websites worldwide.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (tiered shared and cloud hosting plans)
enrichment.seo_title: SiteGround Review 2026: Hosting Plans, Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SiteGround is a Bulgaria-based web host serving millions of domains. Compare 2026 shared, cloud, and WordPress hosting pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SiteGround is a web hosting company founded in 2004 by Ivo Tzenov and a group of university friends in Sofia, Bulgaria, where the company remains headquartered today alongside additional offices in Plovdiv and Stara Zagora, Bulgaria, and Madrid, Spain. Over two decades, SiteGround has grown into one of the most recognized independent web hosts, providing hosting for more than 3 million domains as of 2023 and employing an estimated 500 to 1,000 people. The company offers shared hosting, managed WordPress and WooCommerce hosting, cloud hosting, and enterprise solutions, running its infrastructure on Google Cloud Platform data centers across multiple regions. SiteGround's entry-level shared hosting plans are StartUp, one website with 10 GB storage at a promotional price around $2.99 per month; GrowBig, unlimited websites with 20 GB storage and staging tools at roughly $4.99 per month promotional; and GoGeek, 40 GB storage with priority support and advanced Git and staging tools at roughly $7.99 per month promotional. Renewal prices after the first term are notably higher than these promotional rates. Managed Cloud Hosting starts at roughly $100 per month for dedicated resources. SiteGround is widely known for being an officially recommended WordPress.org host, for free daily backups, its own SG Optimizer caching plugin, free CDN and SSL, and 24/7 human support via chat, phone, and tickets. It competes with Bluehost, Hostinger, and WP Engine in the managed hosting space.

long_form_blocks.heading: SiteGround Overview
paragraphs: SiteGround was founded in 2004 in Sofia, Bulgaria, by Ivo Tzenov and a group of university friends, and it has grown from a small local hosting provider into one of the best-known independent web hosts globally, now serving millions of domains.

The company is headquartered in Sofia with additional offices in Plovdiv and Stara Zagora, Bulgaria, and Madrid, Spain, and employs an estimated 500 to 1,000 people across shared hosting, cloud infrastructure, and managed WordPress services.

SiteGround runs its hosting infrastructure on Google Cloud Platform data centers in multiple regions, which the company uses to market faster load times and better uptime compared with hosts running on older, non-cloud infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SiteGround
paragraphs: SiteGround is an officially recommended host on WordPress.org and provides deep WordPress and WooCommerce optimization, including its proprietary SG Optimizer caching plugin, automatic software updates, and staging environments for testing changes safely.
Every plan includes free daily backups, a free SSL certificate, and a free content delivery network, features that many competitors charge extra for or reserve for higher tiers.

Support is delivered 24/7 through live chat, phone, and ticketing with human agents, and SiteGround's proprietary Site Tools dashboard replaces cPanel with a custom, streamlined control panel interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: SiteGround Pricing Plans
paragraphs: SiteGround's three shared hosting tiers are StartUp, for a single website with 10 GB of storage; GrowBig, which supports unlimited websites, 20 GB of storage, and staging tools; and GoGeek, which adds 40 GB of storage, priority support, and advanced Git integration.

Promotional first-term pricing starts around $2.99 per month for StartUp, $4.99 per month for GrowBig, and $7.99 per month for GoGeek, but renewal pricing after the first billing term increases significantly, a common industry practice worth budgeting for.

Beyond shared hosting, SiteGround offers Managed Cloud Hosting starting around $100 per month for dedicated resources on Google Cloud, aimed at higher-traffic sites and businesses that have outgrown shared hosting.

features.title: Google Cloud infrastructure
features.description: Hosting runs on Google Cloud Platform data centers across multiple regions for performance and reliability.

features.title: Staging and Git tools
features.description: GrowBig and GoGeek plans include staging environments and Git integration for safer development workflows.

features.title: WordPress.org recommended host
features.description: SiteGround is one of a small number of hosts officially recommended by WordPress.org.

features.title: 24/7 human support
features.description: Live chat, phone, and ticket support are staffed around the clock.

features.title: Managed WooCommerce hosting
features.description: Optimized hosting configurations specifically tuned for WooCommerce online stores.

pros: Fast, reliable performance on Google Cloud infrastructure
Strong reputation for responsive customer support
Free daily backups included on all plans
Deep WordPress and WooCommerce optimizations
Choice of data center regions for global audiences
cons: Renewal prices are significantly higher than promotional first-term rates
Storage caps are lower than some flat-rate competitors
No unlimited storage even on the top shared hosting tier
Proprietary Site Tools panel has a learning curve for cPanel veterans
Cloud hosting tier is comparatively expensive versus shared hosting

faqs.question: Is SiteGround good for WordPress?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiteGround is an officially recommended WordPress.org host with built-in optimization tools, staging environments, and automatic updates.

faqs.question: What happens after the SiteGround promotional pricing period ends?
faqs.answer: Plans renew at a higher standard rate after the initial promotional term, which is typically the first year or the length of the initial signup period.

faqs.question: Does SiteGround offer a money-back guarantee?
faqs.answer: SiteGround offers a refund policy on new hosting signups, though the exact window can vary by plan; check the current terms at signup.

faqs.question: What is the difference between shared and cloud hosting at SiteGround?
faqs.answer: Shared hosting plans share server resources among multiple customers at a lower price, while Cloud Hosting provides dedicated resources for higher-traffic sites at a higher price.

faqs.question: Does SiteGround include a free domain?
faqs.answer: SiteGround does not always include a free domain; domain registration or transfer may be a separate cost depending on the current promotion.

faqs.question: Where are SiteGround's data centers located?
faqs.answer: SiteGround operates data centers in multiple global regions on Google Cloud Platform, letting customers choose a location closer to their audience.

faqs.question: Does SiteGround help migrate existing websites?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiteGround provides free or assisted website migration tools and services depending on the plan.

faqs.question: What uptime does SiteGround guarantee?
faqs.answer: SiteGround advertises a 99.9 percent uptime commitment backed by service credits, consistent with common industry standards.

use_cases.title: Growing ecommerce store on WooCommerce

enrichment.short_description: Siter.io is a collaborative, no-code website builder built for designers, letting teams import Figma designs and turn them into live, responsive websites.
enrichment.seo_title: Siter.io Review 2026: Pricing and Features for Designers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Siter.io is a no-code website builder for designers with Figma import and real-time collaboration. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Siter.io is a cloud-based, no-code website builder launched in 2022 by Andrian Valeanu, the founder of Designmodo, a New York-based digital product studio that also built the tool. Siter.io is aimed specifically at designers and design-led teams rather than general small-business owners, differentiating itself from builders like Wix or SITE123 by supporting direct import of Figma designs, which it converts into responsive, publishable websites without requiring the user to write code. The editor combines a visual, layer-based canvas with real-time multiplayer collaboration, letting multiple teammates edit the same project simultaneously, similar to Figma's own collaboration model. Siter.io offers a free Start plan with unlimited websites and teammates but capped at 10 pages and 3 forms, and paid tiers named Solo at $7 per month for one website, Plus at $12 per month for three websites and 50 pages, and Pro at $24 per month for five websites with unlimited pages and forms plus HTML export. All paid plans include hosting, SSL certificates, custom domains, SEO controls, page-speed optimization, and custom fonts. Because Siter.io exports clean HTML on its top tier, agencies and freelance designers can use it to prototype and ship client sites without handing off to a developer for basic builds. As a newer entrant, detailed company size and funding figures are not publicly disclosed, but it operates under the Designmodo umbrella, which has built design tools and marketplaces since the 2010s.

long_form_blocks.heading: Siter.io Overview
paragraphs: Siter.io launched in 2022, created by Andrian Valeanu, founder of the New York-based design studio Designmodo. It was built to give designers a way to turn existing design work into live websites without needing a developer or writing code.

Unlike general-purpose website builders aimed at small businesses, Siter.io is positioned toward designers and design-led teams who already work in tools like Figma and want a direct path from design file to published site.

The product combines a visual, layer-based editor with real-time multiplayer collaboration, letting multiple team members work on the same website project simultaneously, similar to how design teams already collaborate in Figma.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Siter.io
paragraphs: The standout feature is direct Figma import, which converts existing Figma frames and layouts into an editable, publishable website structure, saving designers from rebuilding a design from scratch in a separate tool.

Real-time collaboration is available on every plan, including the free tier, and lets unlimited teammates work within the same account regardless of plan level, with website count and page limits being the main constraints that scale with price.

Paid plans add custom domain connection, built-in SEO controls, page-speed optimization tools, and custom font uploads, and the top Pro plan adds clean HTML export for developers who want to take the generated code elsewhere.

long_form_blocks.heading: Siter.io Pricing Plans
paragraphs: The free Start plan supports unlimited websites and unlimited teammates but is capped at 10 pages and 3 forms per project, and does not include a custom domain, making it best suited for testing or very small sites.

The Solo plan costs $7 per month for one website with a custom domain and 10 pages, while the Plus plan costs $12 per month and raises the limit to three websites and 50 pages, adding room for small agencies managing a handful of client sites.

The Pro plan costs $24 per month, supports five websites with unlimited pages and forms, and is the only tier that includes HTML export, letting teams hand off a finished export to a development workflow if needed.

features.title: Real-time multiplayer editing
features.description: Multiple teammates can edit the same website project simultaneously on any plan, including free.

features.title: Custom domain support
features.description: Paid plans let users connect a custom domain to their published site.

features.title: Built-in SEO controls
features.description: Meta tags and other on-page SEO settings are editable directly within the builder.

features.title: Page speed optimization tools
features.description: Built-in tools help optimize published sites for faster load times.

features.title: Custom font uploads
features.description: Users can upload and use custom fonts beyond the built-in font library.

features.title: HTML export
features.description: The Pro plan allows exporting clean HTML for use outside the Siter.io platform.

features.title: Unlimited teammates on all plans
features.description: Team collaboration is not gated by plan tier, only website and page counts are.

Real-time collaboration included on every plan, even free
Generous free plan for small or test projects
Affordable entry-level paid tier compared to enterprise design tools
Clean HTML export available on the Pro plan
cons: Fewer built-in ecommerce features than mainstream builders like Wix
Smaller template and plugin ecosystem given its newer launch
Newer product with a smaller established user community
Advanced features like HTML export are gated to the top plan
Public company size and roadmap transparency are limited

faqs.question: Is Siter.io free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Siter.io offers a free Start plan with unlimited websites and teammates, though pages and forms are capped and no custom domain is included.

faqs.question: Can I import a Figma file directly into Siter.io?
faqs.answer: Yes, Siter.io supports direct Figma import, converting Figma layouts into an editable website structure.

faqs.question: Does Siter.io support ecommerce?
faqs.answer: Siter.io is primarily a design-focused website builder and does not offer the deep ecommerce features found in dedicated store platforms.

faqs.question: What is included in the Siter.io Pro plan?
faqs.answer: The Pro plan costs $24 per month and includes five websites, unlimited pages and forms, and HTML export.

faqs.question: Who built Siter.io?
faqs.answer: Siter.io was launched in 2022 by Andrian Valeanu, founder of the New York-based design studio Designmodo.

faqs.question: Can I export my Siter.io site as HTML?
faqs.answer: Yes, HTML export is available exclusively on the Pro plan.

faqs.question: Does Siter.io support custom domains?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom domain connection is available starting on the Solo plan.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use Siter.io?
faqs.answer: No, Siter.io is a no-code tool, though it is built with designers in mind and integrates closely with Figma.

enrichment.short_description: SiYuan is a free, open-source, privacy-first personal knowledge management app that combines block-based editing, Markdown, and bidirectional linking, similar to Notion or Obsidian.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source, with optional paid PRO features and cloud subscription
enrichment.starting_price: Free (core app); PRO features from $64 one-time
enrichment.seo_title: SiYuan Review 2026: Free Open-Source Note-Taking App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: SiYuan is a free, open-source, privacy-first personal knowledge management app with block references and bidirectional links. See 2026 features and pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: SiYuan is a free, open-source, privacy-first personal knowledge management application developed by the team behind B3log, a long-running open source community whose creators have described themselves as full-time open source developers since 2018. SiYuan is self-hostable and offline-first: notes are stored locally in a human-readable format that mixes Markdown files with JSON metadata, so users retain full ownership and portability of their data rather than being locked into a proprietary cloud database. The application is released under the AGPL-3.0 license, with its source code publicly hosted on GitHub, where the project has accumulated more than 45,000 stars, reflecting a substantial and active open-source community. Functionally, SiYuan sits alongside tools like Notion, Obsidian, and Logseq: every paragraph, list item, or heading is a discrete block with its own unique ID, which can be referenced or transcluded anywhere else in a user's notebook, enabling bidirectional linking and a network-style graph view of ideas rather than a flat folder hierarchy. Core editing, block references, Markdown support, and offline access are free for both personal and commercial use, on a lifetime basis. Paid tiers exist for convenience features rather than core functionality: a one-time PRO purchase around $64, discounted from $96, unlocks third-party S3 and WebDAV sync and backup integration, while a subscription around $148 per year, discounted from $296, adds official cloud asset hosting, 8GB of official cloud storage, and a cloud inbox. SiYuan ships native apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux, plus mobile apps for Android and iOS, and a browser extension for web clipping.

long_form_blocks.heading: SiYuan Overview
paragraphs: SiYuan is an open-source personal knowledge management application developed by the B3log team, a group that has been building open source software since 2009 and working full-time on open source projects since 2018. The app is released under the AGPL-3.0 license with its full source code public on GitHub.

The core philosophy behind SiYuan is privacy and data ownership: notes are stored locally as Markdown files paired with JSON metadata, meaning users can read and move their data without relying on a proprietary format or a mandatory cloud account.

SiYuan has attracted a large and active open-source community, with its GitHub repository surpassing 45,000 stars, and it ships as native desktop apps for Windows, macOS, and Linux plus mobile apps for Android and iOS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of SiYuan
paragraphs: At the core of SiYuan is block-level referencing: every piece of content, whether a paragraph, heading, or list item, is a discrete block with a unique ID that can be linked or embedded anywhere else in a user's notes, enabling true bidirectional linking rather than simple page-to-page links.

A graph view visualizes the connections between blocks and documents, helping users see how their notes relate to one another as a network rather than a flat folder structure, a workflow similar to Obsidian's graph view.

Because sync is not tied to a mandatory proprietary server, users can sync their notes using third-party services like WebDAV, S3-compatible storage, or Resilio Sync, or opt into SiYuan's own official cloud sync service for convenience.

long_form_blocks.heading: SiYuan Pricing Plans
paragraphs: The core SiYuan application is free for both personal and commercial use, on a lifetime basis, and includes the full block-editing, Markdown, and offline feature set with no artificial limits on note count or local usage.

A one-time PRO purchase, priced around $64 (discounted from $96), unlocks integration with third-party sync and backup services such as Amazon S3 and WebDAV, useful for users who want automated sync without relying on SiYuan's own cloud.

A recurring Subscription plan, priced around $148 per year (discounted from $296), adds SiYuan's official cloud sync service, 8GB of official cloud storage, cloud asset hosting, and a cloud inbox for users who prefer a fully managed sync experience.

features.title: Block-level bidirectional linking
features.description: Every block has a unique ID that can be referenced or transcluded anywhere else in a user's notes.

features.title: Local-first Markdown and JSON storage
features.description: Notes are stored locally in a readable Markdown plus JSON format rather than a proprietary database.

features.title: Optional encrypted cloud sync
features.description: An official paid cloud sync service is available for users who do not want to configure third-party sync.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native apps are available for Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS.

features.title: Graph view
features.description: Visualizes connections between notes and blocks as an interactive network.

features.title: Web clipper extension
features.description: A browser extension captures web content directly into SiYuan notebooks.

features.title: Plugin and theme support
features.description: The app supports community plugins and themes to extend functionality and appearance.

features.title: Self-hosting supported
features.description: Users can self-host sync using WebDAV, S3-compatible storage, or Resilio Sync instead of a paid cloud service.

pros: Fully free and open source core application
Strong data ownership with local Markdown and JSON storage
Active open-source community with over 45,000 GitHub stars
Works fully offline with no forced cloud dependency
Flexible self-hosted sync options via WebDAV or S3
cons: Community and some documentation lean toward Chinese-language users
Steeper learning curve than mainstream consumer note apps
Smaller plugin ecosystem than more established tools like Obsidian
Official cloud sync requires a paid PRO purchase or subscription
Company backing and long-term roadmap are less publicly documented than VC-backed competitors

faqs.question: Is SiYuan really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core application with block editing, Markdown support, and offline access is free for both personal and commercial use.

faqs.question: Is SiYuan open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiYuan is fully open source and released under the AGPL-3.0 license with its code public on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does SiYuan work offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiYuan is offline-first and stores all notes locally by default.

faqs.question: What is a block reference in SiYuan?
faqs.answer: A block reference lets any paragraph, heading, or list item be linked to or embedded in another location in a user's notes, enabling bidirectional linking.

faqs.question: Can I self-host sync for SiYuan?
faqs.answer: Yes, users can sync notes using third-party services like WebDAV, S3-compatible storage, or Resilio Sync instead of paying for official cloud sync.

faqs.question: Is SiYuan available in English?
faqs.answer: Yes, SiYuan supports an English interface alongside Chinese and other languages.

faqs.question: Is my data private with SiYuan?
faqs.answer: SiYuan stores data locally by default and offers optional encrypted cloud sync, giving users control over where their data lives.

faqs.question: How does SiYuan compare to Obsidian or Notion?
faqs.answer: Like Obsidian, SiYuan is local-first and privacy-focused, but it uses native block-level references similar to Notion rather than page-only linking.

use_cases.title: Personal knowledge base with linked notes
use_cases.description: An individual builds a personal knowledge base using block references and bidirectional links to connect related ideas over time.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious self-hosted note sync
use_cases.description: A privacy-conscious user syncs notes across devices using self-hosted WebDAV or S3 storage instead of a proprietary cloud service.

use_cases.title: Research organization with block-level references
use_cases.description: A researcher organizes findings and references at the block level across many interconnected documents.

enrichment.short_description: Sked Social is an Australian social media scheduling and management platform built for growing brands, agencies, and multi-location businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Sked Social Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sked Social is an Australian social media scheduling platform for brands and agencies. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sked Social is a social media scheduling and management platform founded in 2013 and headquartered in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. The platform serves solo social media managers, marketing teams, and agencies that need to plan, approve, and publish content across multiple social channels, including Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, and Google Business Profile. Sked Social differentiates itself with agency-focused workflow features such as customizable client approval portals, white-label reporting, and unified social inbox messaging, alongside standard scheduling, content calendars, and analytics. As of recent public data, the company reports over $10 million in annual revenue and roughly 10,000 customers, while employing an estimated 11 to 50 people, with several public counts placing the team at around 20 to 26 staff spread across multiple continents. Pricing runs on four tiers: Basic at $24 per month, billed annually at $290 per year, for a single user and one profile per platform; Grow at $58 per month, billed annually at $690 per year, for 3 users and 6 profiles with optional approval workflow add-ons; Accelerate at $166 per month, billed annually at $1,990 per year, for 6 users and 10 profiles with approvals and advanced analytics included; and a Custom tier starting from $249 per month for enterprise-level access controls and dedicated account management. Every plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required, brand-trained AI caption generation, ideas and draft management, and mobile apps for iOS and Android.

long_form_blocks.heading: Sked Social Overview
paragraphs: Sked Social was founded in 2013 and is headquartered in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia. It has grown into a social media scheduling platform used by solo social media managers up through agencies managing many client brands at once.

The platform supports scheduling and publishing across major channels including Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, and Google Business Profile, positioning it as a broad, multi-channel scheduler rather than a single-platform tool.

Sked Social operates with a lean team, publicly estimated at roughly 11 to 50 employees spread across multiple continents, while reporting over $10 million in annual revenue and serving a customer base in the range of 10,000 businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Sked Social
paragraphs: Sked Social is particularly known for agency-oriented workflow tools, including customizable client approval portals and a review process that lets external stakeholders approve content without needing a full account login.
A unified social inbox consolidates messages and comments from connected platforms into one place, while brand-trained AI helps generate on-brand captions faster than writing each post manually.

The platform also includes visual content calendars, social listening features, advanced analytics with exportable reports, and native mobile apps for iOS and Android so teams can manage content on the go.

long_form_blocks.heading: Sked Social Pricing Plans
paragraphs: The Basic plan costs $24 per month, billed annually at $290 per year, and includes one user and one profile per connected platform, making it suited to a single social media manager handling one brand.

The Grow plan costs $58 per month, billed annually at $690 per year, and expands to 3 users and 6 profiles, with internal and external approval workflows available as paid add-ons for teams that need review steps.

The Accelerate plan costs $166 per month, billed annually at $1,990 per year, and includes approvals and advanced analytics by default for 6 users and 10 profiles, while a Custom plan starting from $249 per month covers enterprise needs like dedicated account management and tailored access controls.

features.title: Multi-platform scheduling
features.description: Schedule and publish to Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, and Google Business Profile from one dashboard.

features.title: Brand-trained AI captions
features.description: AI-generated captions trained on a brand's voice help speed up content creation.

features.title: Client approval workflows
features.description: Customizable review portals let internal or external stakeholders approve content before it publishes.

features.title: Unified social inbox
features.description: Consolidates messages and comments from connected platforms into a single inbox.

features.title: Advanced analytics with exports
features.description: Reporting includes exportable data, useful for agencies delivering client reports.

features.title: Visual content calendar
features.description: A calendar view lets teams plan and rearrange scheduled content visually.

features.title: Social listening
features.description: Tracks brand mentions and relevant conversations across connected platforms.

features.title: Mobile apps
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps allow scheduling and approvals from mobile devices.

pros: Strong agency and multi-brand approval workflow tools
Broad platform coverage including TikTok and Google Business Profile
AI-assisted caption writing trained on brand voice
14-day free trial with no credit card required
Responsive customer support team
cons: Higher-tier plans become expensive as profile and user counts grow
Approval workflow add-ons cost extra on the Grow plan
Steeper learning curve for full agency feature set
Best pricing requires annual billing commitment
Extra users and profiles add incremental per-year costs

faqs.question: What platforms does Sked Social support?
faqs.answer: Sked Social supports Instagram, TikTok, Facebook, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, and Google Business Profile, among others.

faqs.question: Does Sked Social offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, every plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Grow and Accelerate plans?
faqs.answer: Grow includes 3 users and 6 profiles with approval workflows as paid add-ons, while Accelerate includes 6 users, 10 profiles, and approvals plus advanced analytics by default.

faqs.question: Does Sked Social support client approvals?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sked Social offers customizable internal and external approval portals, a key feature for agencies.

faqs.question: Is there a free plan for Sked Social?
faqs.answer: No, Sked Social does not offer an ongoing free plan, but it does offer a 14-day free trial.

faqs.question: Can I add extra social profiles to my Sked Social plan?
faqs.answer: Yes, additional profiles can be added to Grow and Accelerate plans for an extra annual fee per profile.

faqs.question: Is Sked Social good for agencies?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sked Social is widely used by agencies due to its client approval portals, white-label reporting, and multi-brand management tools.

faqs.question: Where is Sked Social headquartered?
faqs.answer: Sked Social was founded in 2013 and is headquartered in Melbourne, Victoria, Australia.

use_cases.title: Agency managing approvals across client brands

use_cases.description: An individual social media manager uses the Basic plan to schedule and publish content for a single brand across multiple platforms.

enrichment.short_description: Sketch is a vector-based digital design tool for Mac used by UI/UX designers to create interfaces, prototypes, and design systems, made by Sketch B.V.
enrichment.pricing_model: Subscription (per-editor, annual or monthly) plus a one-time Mac-only license option
enrichment.seo_title: Sketch Review 2026: Pricing, Features for UI/UX Design
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Sketch is a Mac-based UI/UX design tool from Sketch B.V. See 2026 pricing plans, features, pros and cons for designers and teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Sketch is a vector-based digital design application originally created in 2010 by Pieter Omvlee and a small team working under the name Bohemian Coding in the Netherlands; the product and company are now operated as Sketch B.V. Sketch was one of the first mainstream tools to shift UI and UX design away from Photoshop toward a purpose-built, vector-first workflow, and it helped popularize concepts like reusable symbols and shared design libraries that later spread across the design-tool category, including competitors like Figma and Adobe XD. The application remains a native macOS app, complemented by a browser-based web app and mobile companion apps for previewing designs on iPhone and iPad, plus real-time multiplayer collaboration, version history, and developer handoff tools for exporting specs and assets. Sketch is sold on a per-editor subscription: the Standard plan costs $12 per editor per month when billed annually and includes real-time collaboration, unlimited documents, and unlimited free viewers; Professional costs $24 per editor per month and adds single sign-on, project archiving, and permission groups; Enterprise costs $44 per editor per month and adds SCIM provisioning and bring-your-own-key encryption; and a custom-priced Private Cloud tier offers a fully private hosted environment. Sketch also still sells a one-time $120 Mac-only license, including one year of updates, for freelancers who want a native offline app without collaboration features. The company employs roughly 100 people.

long_form_blocks.heading: Sketch Overview
paragraphs: Sketch was created in 2010 by Pieter Omvlee and a small team originally operating under the name Bohemian Coding in the Netherlands. The product and company are today known as Sketch B.V.

Sketch is widely credited as one of the tools that shifted mainstream interface design away from raster tools like Photoshop toward a dedicated, vector-first design workflow, introducing concepts such as reusable symbols that influenced how the entire UI design tool category evolved.

The application remains a native macOS app at its core, supplemented over time by a browser-based web app and mobile companion apps, as the company adapted to competition from fully browser-based tools like Figma.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Sketch
paragraphs: Sketch's editor is built around reusable symbols and shared libraries, letting design teams maintain consistent components, such as buttons or icons, across many files from a single source of truth.
Real-time multiplayer collaboration lets multiple designers work in the same document simultaneously, with version history preserving past states of a file for easy rollback.

Developer handoff tools let engineers inspect exact spacing, colors, and assets directly from a design file, and Sketch supports a broad plugin ecosystem that extends the editor with third-party functionality.

long_form_blocks.heading: Sketch Pricing Plans
paragraphs: The Standard plan costs $12 per editor per month when billed annually and includes real-time collaboration, unlimited documents, and unlimited free viewers, making it the entry point for small teams.

The Professional plan costs $24 per editor per month and adds single sign-on, project archiving, and permission groups, while Enterprise costs $44 per editor per month and adds SCIM provisioning and bring-your-own-key encryption for larger organizations with stricter security needs.

For designers who prefer not to subscribe, Sketch still sells a one-time Mac-only license for $120 per seat, which includes one year of updates but excludes collaboration features, online sharing, and the mobile preview app.

features.title: Native macOS vector editor
features.description: A purpose-built, vector-first design app native to Mac, optimized for interface design work.

features.title: Reusable symbols and shared libraries
features.description: Design components can be reused and kept consistent across files through shared libraries.

features.title: Real-time multiplayer collaboration
features.description: Multiple designers can edit the same document simultaneously on subscription plans.

features.title: Developer handoff tools
features.description: Engineers can inspect exact specs, colors, and export assets directly from design files.

features.title: Version history
features.description: Past states of a document are preserved and can be restored.

features.title: Web app and mobile companion apps
features.description: Designs can be viewed and reviewed via a browser-based app and iPhone or iPad companion apps.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: A wide range of third-party plugins extend Sketch's default functionality.

features.title: Prototyping tools
features.description: Built-in prototyping lets designers link artboards together to simulate user flows.

pros: Mature, polished native Mac performance
Strong plugin and shared library ecosystem
Flexible one-time license option for freelancers
Solid developer handoff tooling
Long track record in the UI/UX design industry since 2010
cons: Native app is Mac-only, unlike fully cross-platform competitors
Lost significant market share to Figma's browser-based collaboration model
Per-editor pricing adds up for larger design teams
Real-time collaboration only available on subscription plans, not the one-time license
Enterprise-level features cost significantly more than the base plan

faqs.question: Is Sketch only available for Mac?
faqs.answer: The native Sketch app is Mac-only, though a web app and mobile companion apps are also available for viewing and light editing.

faqs.question: Can I try Sketch for free?
faqs.answer: Sketch subscription plans include a 30-day free trial; the one-time Mac-only license does not include a free trial.

faqs.question: What is the difference between the Mac-only license and a subscription?
faqs.answer: The Mac-only license is a one-time $120 purchase for the native app without collaboration features, while subscriptions add real-time collaboration, the web app, and ongoing updates.

faqs.question: Does Sketch support real-time collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, real-time multiplayer collaboration is available on all subscription plans.

faqs.question: How does Sketch compare to Figma?
faqs.answer: Sketch is a native Mac app with an optional web component, while Figma is fully browser-based; Figma has become more dominant for real-time team collaboration, while Sketch retains a following for native Mac performance.

faqs.question: Does Sketch offer a web app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Sketch includes a browser-based web app alongside its native macOS application.

faqs.question: What is included in the Sketch Professional plan?
faqs.answer: Professional costs $24 per editor per month and adds single sign-on, project archiving, and permission groups on top of the Standard plan.

faqs.question: Can teams use single sign-on with Sketch?
faqs.answer: Yes, single sign-on is available starting on the Professional plan.

use_cases.title: UI/UX designer building app interfaces natively on Mac
use_cases.description: A product designer uses Sketch's native macOS app to design and prototype mobile and web interfaces.

use_cases.title: Design team collaborating on a shared design system
use_cases.description: A design team uses shared libraries and real-time collaboration to maintain a consistent design system across many projects.

use_cases.title: Freelance designer preferring a one-time license
use_cases.description: A freelance designer purchases the one-time Mac-only license instead of a recurring subscription for occasional project work.

pricing_plans.billing_cycle: One-time purchase, 1 year of updates included

enrichment.short_description: Skillshare is an online learning community offering thousands of video classes in creative fields like design, illustration, photography, and business.
enrichment.seo_title: Skillshare Review 2026: Pricing, Classes and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Skillshare is an online learning platform with thousands of creative classes. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons for individuals and teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Skillshare is an online learning platform founded by Michael Karnjanaprakorn and Malcolm Ong in New York City in November 2010, with the site publicly launching in 2011; the company remains headquartered in New York and today employs an estimated 500 to 700 people. Skillshare's catalog spans more than 30,000 classes, concentrated in creative and entrepreneurial skills such as illustration, graphic design, photography, video editing, writing, marketing, and productivity, taught by working practitioners rather than accredited academic instructors. The platform operates on a community model: most classes are short, project-based video lessons, and students are encouraged to complete a hands-on class project and share it for peer feedback rather than sit exams. Access to the full catalog requires a Premium Membership, priced at $167.88 per year, equivalent to $13.99 per month when billed annually, or a higher month-to-month rate typically between $17.99 and $32; a limited selection of classes remains free without a subscription. New users generally receive a free trial period before billing begins. Skillshare for Teams extends premium access to organizations at $159 per user per year, billed annually only, aimed at companies that want to offer employees ongoing creative and professional-development learning. Skillshare competes with platforms like Coursera, Udemy, MasterClass, and LinkedIn Learning, differentiating itself through project-based classes and a community-oriented, creator-led catalog rather than certificate-driven professional courses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Skillshare Overview
paragraphs: Skillshare was founded by Michael Karnjanaprakorn and Malcolm Ong in New York City in November 2010, with the platform publicly launching to users in 2011. The company remains headquartered in New York.

The platform built its identity around creative and entrepreneurial skills rather than accredited academic or technical certification, positioning itself as a community of practitioners teaching practical, project-based classes.

Skillshare today employs an estimated 500 to 700 people and offers a catalog of more than 30,000 classes, spanning design, illustration, photography, video, writing, marketing, and productivity topics.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features of Skillshare
paragraphs: Most Skillshare classes are structured around a hands-on class project, encouraging students to apply what they learn immediately and share their work for feedback from other students, rather than passively watching lectures.

Instructors are typically working creative professionals rather than credentialed academics, which gives the catalog a practical, industry-current feel, though it also means class quality can vary more than on accreditation-focused platforms.
The mobile app supports offline downloads for learning without an internet connection, and Skillshare curates learning paths that group related classes together around a broader skill or career goal.

long_form_blocks.heading: Skillshare Pricing Plans
paragraphs: A limited selection of classes is available for free without a subscription, but full catalog access requires a Premium Membership.

Premium Membership costs $167.88 per year, equivalent to $13.99 per month when billed annually, while paying strictly month-to-month costs more, typically between $17.99 and $32 per month depending on the current offer.
Skillshare for Teams extends premium access to organizations at $159 per user per year, billed annually only, aimed at companies offering Skillshare as an employee learning and development benefit.

features.title: 30,000+ on-demand video classes
features.description: A large, constantly updated catalog spans creative, business, and productivity topics.

features.title: Project-based learning
features.description: Most classes are built around a hands-on project rather than passive video watching.

features.title: Offline viewing in mobile app
features.description: Classes can be downloaded for offline viewing on the Skillshare mobile app.

features.title: Curated learning paths
features.description: Related classes are grouped into paths aimed at a broader skill or career goal.

features.title: Creator-led original content
features.description: Instructors are working creative professionals rather than accredited academic instructors.

features.title: Skillshare for Teams
features.description: A business plan extends premium access to employees as a company-provided learning benefit.

features.title: Free trial for new members
features.description: New users typically receive a free trial period before Premium Membership billing begins.

features.title: Community project feedback
features.description: Students can share completed class projects and receive feedback from other learners.

pros: Large, constantly updated creative class catalog
Affordable annual pricing compared to buying individual courses elsewhere
Project-based format encourages practical skill-building
Offline downloads support mobile learning
Strong focus on creative and entrepreneurial skills
cons: No accredited certificates or degrees offered
Class quality varies since instructors are not academically vetted
Monthly billing costs meaningfully more than annual billing
Limited free content without a paid subscription
Less focused on technical or IT certification tracks than platforms like Coursera or Udemy

faqs.question: Is Skillshare free?
faqs.answer: A limited selection of classes is free, but full catalog access requires a paid Premium Membership.

faqs.question: How much does Skillshare Premium cost?
faqs.answer: Premium Membership costs $167.88 per year, equivalent to $13.99 per month when billed annually; month-to-month billing costs more.

faqs.question: Does Skillshare offer certificates?
faqs.answer: Skillshare does not offer accredited certificates or degrees; it focuses on practical, project-based skill-building instead.

faqs.question: What is Skillshare for Teams?
faqs.answer: Skillshare for Teams is a business plan that gives employees Premium access, priced at $159 per user per year, billed annually.

faqs.question: Can I cancel my Skillshare subscription anytime?
faqs.answer: Yes, Premium Membership can generally be canceled at any time, though refund policies depend on the current terms at signup.

faqs.question: Does Skillshare offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, new members typically receive a free trial period before Premium Membership billing begins.

faqs.question: What subjects does Skillshare cover?
faqs.answer: Skillshare focuses on creative and entrepreneurial skills including illustration, design, photography, video, writing, marketing, and productivity.

faqs.question: Is Skillshare good for professional certification?
faqs.answer: No, Skillshare is better suited for practical creative skill-building than for accredited professional certification, unlike platforms such as Coursera.

use_cases.description: A freelance illustrator or designer takes project-based classes to learn new techniques and build a portfolio piece.

use_cases.title: Small business owner learning marketing skills
